content.full_text:
RAVI MEHTA
CHAIRMAN

FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION

August 23, 1996

Ms. Julia A. Moll
Deputy City Attorney
Office of the City Attorney
City and County of San Francisco
Fox Plaza
1390 Market Street, Fifth Floor
San Francisco, California 94102-5408

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-96-236

Dear Ms. Moll:

This is in response to your request for advice regarding the provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1

QUESTIONS

1. Under the Act, may a government employee properly accept a round-trip airline ticket from a school in order to participate in a fellowship program?

2. May the employee properly accept the program's offer to pay for the employee's meals, lodging and other related expenses while visiting the institution during the three to four day fellowship?

3. May the employee ask the program to donate the $1,000 to $2,000 honorarium associated with the fellowship to a charitable foundation under the facts described below?

CONCLUSIONS

1. Under the Act, the employee may accept the airline ticket because the travel is reasonably related to a governmental purpose or issue of public policy, and is provided by a bona fide public or private educational institution.

1 Government Code Sections 81000-91015. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, Sections 18000-18995 of the California Code of Regulations.

428 J STREET, SUITE 800, SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814 (916) 322-5660

File No. A-96-236
Page 2


2. The employee may accept payment from the law school for
lodging and subsistence in connection with this travel.

3. The employee is prohibited from accepting an honorarium
from any source that the employee would be required to report the
receipt of income or gifts from on his statement of economic
interests. The employee may donate the honorarium to a charitable
organization pursuant to the requirements of Regulation 18932.5.

FACTS

A law school of national stature on the East Coast has
selected a government employee for a public interest fellowship.
The law school is a bona fide educational institution. The
employee is designated under the City of San Francisco's conflict
of interest code to file periodic statements of economic
interests. Neither the employee nor his agency make or
participate in decisions affecting the law school.

The fellowship program brings lawyers who practice in the
public interest to the school for two to four day periods. The
purpose is for experienced public interest lawyers to discuss with
students their aspirations and concerns about public interest
legal careers. Participation in the program will benefit the
government by exposing future lawyers to opportunities to work
in government generally, and more particularly, to work in the
government agency in which the employee serves.

The fellowship carries an honorarium of $1,000 to $2,000. In
addition, the program pays for the fellows' airline travel and
related expenses, including meals and lodging, over a three to
four day period. The school has invited the employee to
participate in the program beginning on September 18, 1996.

Rather than accept the honorarium, the employee would like to
ask the program to donate the honorarium to a tax-exempt,
charitable foundation. The employee serves as an officer of the
foundation and is on its board. The employee has no financial
interest in the foundation and receives no financial benefit from
the foundation, although he has taken charitable deductions in the
past (and may take them in the future) for contributing his own
funds to the foundation. In this instance, the employee would
claim no tax benefit or other benefit from the program's donation
of the honorarium. The donation would not pass through the hands
of the employee.

ANALYSIS

1. Travel Payments

Most payments for travel or free travel provided to public
officials by third parties constitute gifts to the public
officials subject to the $280 gift limit. Under Section 89506,

File No. A-96-236
Page 3


however, an exception exists for travel that is paid for by a bona
fide educational organization and is related to a governmental
purpose or an issue of public policy.

     Section 89506(a)(2) provides as follows:

          (a)  Payments, advances, or reimbursements, for
travel, including actual transportation and related
lodging and subsistence which is reasonably related to a
legislative or governmental purpose, or to an issue of
state, national, or international public policy, are not
prohibited or limited by this chapter if either of the
following apply:

* * *

          (2) The travel is provided by a government, a
governmental agency, a foreign government, a
governmental authority, a bona fide public or private
educational institution, as defined in Section 203 of
the Revenue and Taxation Code, a nonprofit charitable or
religious organization which is exempt from taxation
under Section 501(c)(3) of the Internal Revenue Code, or
by a person domiciled outside the United States which
substantially satisfies the requirements for tax-exempt
status under Section 501(c)(3) of the Internal Revenue
Code.

* * *

                    (Emphasis added.)

     Section 203(b) of the State Revenue and Taxation Code defines
an "educational institution of collegiate grade" as follows:

          [A]n institution incorporated as a college or
seminary of learning, which requires for regular
admission the completion of a four-year high school
course or its equivalent, and confers upon its graduates
at least one academic or professional degree, based on a
course of at least two years in liberal arts and
sciences, or on a course of at least three years in
professional studies, such as law, theology, education,
medicine, dentistry, engineering, veterinary medicine,
pharmacy, architecture, fine arts, commerce, or
journalism.

* * *

     In this case, the San Francisco city employee's travel will
be paid for by a law school on the East Coast. The law school
brings experienced public interest lawyers to speak with students
about careers in government and public interest law. The travel
is related to a governmental purpose or issue of public policy.

File No. A-96-236
Page 4


Therefore, the employee may accept reimbursement for travel and
related lodging and subsistence expenses from the law school.

2. Honorarium

Under Section 89502(c), a designated employee of a local
government agency may not accept an honorarium from any source
that the employee would be required to report the receipt of
income or gifts from on his or her statement of economic
interests. However, any payments that are donated directly to
charity are not included in the definition of "honorarium."
Regulation 18932.5 provides:

The following payments are excluded from the
definition of "honorarium":

(a) A charitable donation, if all of the
following apply:

(1) The donation is made directly to a bona
fide charitable, educational, civic, religious, or
similar tax-exempt, nonprofit organization, and is
not delivered to the individual;

(2) The individual does not make the donation
a condition for his or her speech, article, or
attendance;

(3) The individual does not claim the
donation as a deduction from income for tax
purposes;

(4) The donation will have no reasonably
foreseeable financial effect on the individual, or
on any member of his or her immediate family; and

(5) The individual is not identified to the
recipient organization in connection with the
donation. If the individual knows or has reason to
know that a donor intends to make such a donation,
the individual must inform the donor that the
donation cannot be made in the individual's name.

In addition to paying the employee's travel expenses, the law
school will pay the employee an honorarium of from $1,000 to
$2,000. The employee plans to ask the law school to donate the
honorarium to a tax-exempt, charitable foundation. The employee
serves as an officer of the foundation and is on its board. The
employee has no financial interest in the foundation and receives
no financial benefit from the foundation. The employee would not
claim a tax benefit or other benefit from the donation of the
honorarium, and the donation would be made directly from the law
school's program to the foundation.

File No. A-96-236
Page 5


     The employee may ask the law school to make the donation to
the foundation as long as the requirements set forth above are
complied with.  Under Regulation 18932.5(a)(4), the employee may
make the donation to the foundation as long as the donation will
not have a reasonably foreseeable financial effect on him.²
In addition, under subdivision (a)(5), the employee may not be
identified to the foundation in connection with the donation.

     I trust this answers your question.  If you have any further
questions regarding this matter, please contact me at 916/322-
5660.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        Steven G. Churchwell
                                        General Counsel

                                        [signature]
                                        By: Hyla P. Wagner
                                            Counsel, Legal Division

SGC:HPW:ak




_________________________
²   Section 89519(c) contains a similar provision regarding the
donation of surplus campaign funds.  Interpreting this provision,
we have advised that an officeholder may donate surplus campaign
funds to a nonprofit organization, even though he serves on its
board of directors, if the donation does not benefit him
financially.  (Spillane Advice Letter, No. A-95-071; see also,
Roche Advice Letter, No. G-95-112, and Poster Advice Letter, No.
A-93-247.)
